notes:
- Key concepts introduced (with brief explanation)
  - Assistant agent: an agent designed to assist with tasks; can have a model (“brain”), multiple tools, a system message (instruction on behavior), and a model context; typical go-to agent for most use cases (claimed 80–99%).
  - Tools for agents: functions or integrations agents can call to perform actions (example: web search implemented as a custom function). Tools can be mocked in demos.
  - Brain / Model / model_client: the agent’s LLM component. In Autogen the “brain” was formerly called “model”; you supply a model_client argument to choose the LLM.
  - System message: initial instruction passed into the agent’s brain that guides behavior (example: "use tools to solve task").
  - Description field: optional metadata for an agent (not mandatory).
  - Code executor agent: an agent specialized to execute code; can act like a developer, check code in real time; more complex to write.
  - User proxy agent: an agent that behaves like a user; used for human-in-the-loop flows (sends messages as a user).
  - Society-of-mind agent: an agent that uses an inner team of agents to generate responses (recent addition).
  - Other built-in agents mentioned: message filter conf agent, base chat agent, plus an expanding library of agents.
  - Agent interface in core API: agents are an interface you implement to use them in core Autogen.
  - Stateful agents: agents are expected to maintain state/history internally and accept new messages (follow-ups) rather than requiring the full conversation history each time.
  - Run vs Run-stream / on_message vs on_message_stream:
    - agent.run (async await agent.run): single-call method; output is harder to interpret for production.
    - run_stream / on_message_stream: streaming/evented approach that breaks outputs into readable events (tool call request, tool execution event, assistant final message, summaries); recommended for clarity and multi-agent flows.
  - Message event sequence for a single tool-assisted interaction:
    - User text message (initial user request)
    - Tool call request (agent decides to call a tool and issues a request)
    - Tool execution event (tool runs and returns content)
    - Tool call summary / assistant final message (assistant summarizes and returns final content)
  - Function call ID: unique ID for a tool call; important for logging and matching between events.
  - Parsing outputs for production: it’s critical to interpret and validate agent outputs (don’t assume task is done when you get a response).
  - Mocking tools in demos: demonstration often mocks external calls (e.g., web search) instead of calling real search engines.
  - Agent role potential: agents can be designed to replace human roles (digital human concept) if well-built.

- Any analogies or metaphors used
  - “Best representation of a digital human” — assistant agent compared to a digital human.
  - “Brain” used as metaphor for model/LLM powering the agent.
  - “Society of mind” — agent that uses an inner team of agents (metaphor from cognitive architectures / inner team).
  - Comparison to ChatGPT conversational follow-ups: agents behaving like chat sessions where you can do follow-ups without resending full history.

- Any code, commands, or config shown (literal fragments / descriptions captured)
  - Imports (described verbally):
    - importing the assistant agent
    - importing an OpenAI chat client from model (spoken as “open AI chat condition client from model”)
    - importing messages module
  - Boilerplate: getting the API key and creating a model client (described, not exact code shown).
  - Tool definition example: a custom function acting as a web search (mocked; does not contact Google).
  - Agent definition example (described fields):
    - name: (agent name)
    - brain: model_client
    - tools: [web search]
    - system_message: "use tools to solve task"
    - description: optional (example provided: "an agent which uses tool to help solve task")
  - Running agent:
    - await agent.run(...) (asynchronous run)
    - on_message / on_message_stream (using message-based streaming run)
  - Accessing output:
    - result.messages[-1] (get last message/content)
  - Configuration option:
    - set output_stats = True (to see a summary)

- Any warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - Don’t assume the entire user request is sent verbatim to a tool — the agent sends a distilled/smart query to the tool.
  - You must make sense of outputs for production; obtaining an agent response is not the end — parse, validate, and log.
  - Function call ID must be recorded / used for logging and correlating events — it remains the same across related events.
  - run (non-stream) outputs are harder to interpret; prefer streaming/on_message_stream for clearer, incremental events — especially in multi-agent setups.
  - Code executor agent: more complex to implement and run safely; requires care.
  - Demo tools are often mocked; production should call real services with appropriate safety and logging.
  - Description field is not required — don’t rely on it being present.

- Any comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - Agents mentioned (list candidate):
    - Assistant agent
    - Code executor agent
    - User proxy agent
    - Society-of-mind agent
    - Message filter conf agent
    - Base chat agent
  - Agent attributes / characteristics (list candidate):
    - name
    - description (optional)
    - brain / model_client
    - tools (list)
    - system_message
    - run/run_stream methods
  - Message/event flow for tool usage (list candidate / table rows):
    - 1) User text message
    - 2) Tool call request (with query; function_call_id present)
    - 3) Tool execution event (tool returns content)
    - 4) Tool call summary / Assistant final message (assistant returns consolidated content)
  - Run modes (comparison candidate):
    - run (await agent.run): simpler call, less clear output
    - run_stream / on_message_stream: evented/streaming, clearer breakdown, recommended

- Direct quotes worth preserving
  - "use tools to solve task" (example system message)
  - "Find information about labrad.riever" (example user query — note typo)
  - "always return an answer in JSON." (example instruction given to an agent)
  - "This function call ID. This is very important for your uh logging purposes because this ID will be same here as well."
  - "agent is an interface which you have to implement if you want to use that."
  - "agents are expected to be stateful and this method is expected to call with new message not complete history."
  - "agent chat provides you by default some agents"
  - "best representation of a digital human"
  - "the brain is formerly known as model in autogen and model client is the argument which in which we have to provide which LLM we are going to use."
